overview.body: Last week, you created a City class that knows its own name, connections, and type. But a road trip needs a traveler. This week, you'll create a Game class that tracks the journey — current location, total miles, cities visited. Then you'll make City and Game objects work together. The City displays destinations; the Game tracks your journey. Two classes, one program.

mentorQuote.quote: In Module 4, you had current_city and total_miles as loose variables floating around. Now the Game object owns that state. Ask where you are: game.get_current_city(). Move forward: game.travel_to(...). The object manages itself.

objectives: Private attributes for name, current city, total miles, and cities visited
A game loop where City objects show destinations and Game objects track movement
A win condition when the player reaches a WIN city
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 10 Sections: 10.2 (Classes), 10.3 (Working with Instances), 10.4 (Techniques for Designing Classes)
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 10: Classes and Object-Oriented Programming
programRequirements: Game MUST use private attributes with double underscore prefix
Game MUST track cities visited in a list
lab-5-2.py MUST have a game loop that ends when the player reaches a WIN city
overview.filenameNote: This week you'll work with FOUR files: city.py (your City class from Lab 5.1), game.py (the new Game class you'll build), function_library.py (your utility functions from previous labs), and lab-5-2.py (the game that uses all three modules together).
overview.additionalFilesNote: CodeGrade tests that lab-5-2.py can import all three modules. Missing any file will cause import failures.

rows.task: Complete game loop with win/dead-end logic

timeGuide.strategy: Build the Game class first and test it standalone before trying to integrate with City. Get one class working perfectly, then connect them. Debugging two broken classes at once is a nightmare!

beforeYouBegin.intro: game.py contains nothing but the Game class definition. lab-5-2.py imports City, Game, and get_valid_int, then runs the complete game. Build and test each checkpoint before moving to the next.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- game.py ----
class Game:

    # --- Constructor ---
    __init__(self, name, starting_city)       stores 4 private attributes
        self.__name            = name
        self.__current_city    = starting_city
        self.__total_miles     = 0
        self.__cities_visited  = [starting_city]   start with starting city!

    # --- Getter Methods ---   [CP1]
    get_name(self)                returns self.__name
    get_current_city(self)        returns self.__current_city
    get_total_miles(self)         returns self.__total_miles
    get_cities_visited(self)      returns self.__cities_visited.copy()

    # --- Travel Methods ---   [CP2]
    travel_to(self, city_name, distance)      updates city, miles, visited list

    # --- Display Methods ---   [CP2]
    display_stats(self)           prints team, city, miles, cities count
    __str__(self)                 returns 'Maria in San Antonio'


# ---- lab-5-2.py ----
from city import City
from game import Game
from function_library import get_valid_int

create_cities()    returns dict of connected City objects   [CP3]
main()             title banner, city network, game loop    [CP4]
    while playing:
        current_city = cities[game.get_current_city()]
        check win / dead-end / show destinations
        game.travel_to(city_name, distance)

if __name__ == '__main__': -> main()

checkpoints.title: Game Class Foundation
checkpoints.subtitle: Create game.py with the Game class, __init__ constructor, and getter methods.

content.body: A Game class that stores journey state: team name, current city, total miles traveled, and a list of all cities visited.

Professional games, apps, and systems use a "state object" that carries all the information about what's happening. Instead of scattering variables everywhere, the Game object owns its state. This pattern makes code cleaner, debugging easier, and save/load features possible.

content.quote: When I first learned OOP, the lightbulb moment was realizing that the Game object carries its own history. You don't need global variables to track where you've been — the game knows. Ask it: game.get_cities_visited(). It remembers for you.

references.description: You already know this pattern from Lab 5.1! Game follows the exact same structure — __init__ with private attributes, getter methods for each. The only new thing is that one attribute (__cities_visited) is a list instead of a simple value.

items: Create a new file called game.py
Add the module docstring at the top:

content.code:
"""
game.py
Name: Your Name
abc123: your_abc123
Section: 001
Description: Game class for the Deep in the Heart road trip game.

Chapter 10 Concepts:
- Class definition
- __init__ constructor
- Private attributes
- Getter methods
- Methods that modify attributes
- __str__ method
"""

items: Define the Game class with a docstring explaining the private attributes

Write __init__(self, name, starting_city) that initializes four private attributes: self.__name = name, self.__current_city = starting_city, self.__total_miles = 0, and self.__cities_visited = [starting_city] (start with the starting city already in the list!)
Add getter methods: get_name(), get_current_city(), get_total_miles()
Add get_cities_visited() that returns a copy of the list:

content.body: Step 1: Add this test code at the bottom of game.py:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 2
game = Game('Maria', 'San Antonio')
print('Testing Game getters:')
print(f'  Name: {game.get_name()}')
print(f'  Current City: {game.get_current_city()}')
print(f'  Total Miles: {game.get_total_miles()}')
print(f'  Cities Visited: {game.get_cities_visited()}')

content.output:
Testing Game getters:
  Name: Maria
  Current City: San Antonio
  Total Miles: 0
  Cities Visited: ['San Antonio']

content.body: Step 3: Delete the debug code before continuing.

rows: Returns the actual list — outside code could modify it!
Returns a copy — protects the private data

items: game.py with module docstring
Game class with class docstring
__init__ with 4 private attributes
Four getter methods (returning .copy() for the list)

checkpoints.title: Travel & Display Methods
checkpoints.subtitle: Add methods that modify game state and display information.

content.body: The travel_to() method that updates game state — new city, added miles, and recorded visit. Plus display and __str__ methods.

items: Add a comment # --- Travel Methods ---
Define travel_to(self, city_name, distance):

content.code:
def travel_to(self, city_name, distance):
    """
    Travel to a new city.

    Args:
        city_name (str): Name of destination city
        distance (int): Distance in miles
    """
    self.__current_city = city_name
    self.__total_miles = self.__total_miles + distance
    self.__cities_visited.append(city_name)

items: Add a comment # --- Display Methods ---
Define display_stats(self) that prints game statistics:

content.code:
def display_stats(self):
    """Display game statistics."""
    print(f'Team: {self.__name}')
    print(f'Current City: {self.__current_city}')
    print(f'Total Miles: {self.__total_miles}')
    print(f'Cities Visited: {len(self.__cities_visited)}')

items: Define __str__(self) that returns a short string representation:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 3
game = Game('Maria', 'San Antonio')
print(f'Starting: {game}')
print()

game.travel_to('Houston', 200)
print(f'After Houston: {game}')

game.travel_to('Corpus Christi', 210)
print(f'After Corpus: {game}')
print()

game.display_stats()
print()
print(f'Route: {game.get_cities_visited()}')

content.output:
Starting: Maria in San Antonio

After Houston: Maria in Houston
After Corpus: Maria in Corpus Christi

Team: Maria
Current City: Corpus Christi
Total Miles: 410
Cities Visited: 3

Route: ['San Antonio', 'Houston', 'Corpus Christi']

content.body: Step 3: Delete the debug code before continuing.

rows: __str__ must return a string — don't print inside it
Return a copy with list(self.__cities_visited) to protect the original

items: travel_to() updates city, miles, and visited list
display_stats() prints formatted stats
__str__() returns short string for printing

checkpoints.title: Build the City Network
checkpoints.subtitle: Create lab-5-2.py with a function that builds interconnected City objects.

content.body: A create_cities() function that creates City objects, connects them with roads, and returns a dictionary for easy lookup by name.

items: Create lab-5-2.py with your standard header docstring
Add imports at the top:

content.code:
from city import City
from game import Game
from function_library import get_valid_int

items: Define create_cities() that creates 4 City objects — San Antonio (START), Houston (HUB), Corpus Christi (WIN), and Laredo (DEAD_END) — adds connections between them using add_connection(), and returns a dictionary: {'San Antonio': san_antonio, 'Houston': houston, ...}
Here's the city network to build:

content.code:
def main():
    # DEBUG - will be replaced in Checkpoint 4
    cities = create_cities()

    print('Cities created:')
    for name, city in cities.items():
        print(f'  {city}')

    print()
    print('Testing lookup and connections:')
    sa = cities['San Antonio']
    sa.display_destinations()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

content.output:
Cities created:
  San Antonio (START)
  Houston (HUB)
  Corpus Christi (WIN)
  Laredo (DEAD_END)

Testing lookup and connections:
From San Antonio, you can travel to:
  1. Houston - 200 miles
  2. Corpus Christi - 150 miles
  3. Laredo - 160 miles

rows: Storing string names instead of City objects
Dictionary values should be the City objects themselves
Adding connections before creating all cities
Create ALL City objects first, THEN add connections
If SA connects to Houston, Houston should connect back to SA

items: lab-5-2.py imports City, Game, and get_valid_int
create_cities() returns a dictionary of connected City objects
Can look up any city by name: cities['Houston']

checkpoints.title: Complete Game Loop
checkpoints.subtitle: Build the main() function with a game loop where City and Game work together.

content.body: A complete game loop: get the current City object from the game's location, check for win or dead-end, show destinations, get choice, travel. Two classes working in harmony!

content.quote: Notice how clean the game loop is now. You ask the game where it is, get that city from the dictionary, ask the city what destinations are available, then tell the game to travel. Each object does its own job. That's the OOP magic.

items: Replace your test main() with the real game loop
Start with a title banner, create cities, prompt for team name, and create a Game object
Build the game loop using this structure:

content.code:
playing = True

while playing:
    # Get current city OBJECT from dictionary
    current_city = cities[game.get_current_city()]

    # Display current location
    print()
    print('-' * 40)
    print(f'You are in: {current_city}')
    print(f'"{current_city.get_tagline()}"')
    print('-' * 40)

    # Check for win
    if current_city.is_win_city():
        print()
        print('YOU REACHED THE BEACH!')
        playing = False
    elif current_city.is_dead_end():
        # Send back to San Antonio
        print()
        print('This is a dead end! No beach here.')
        print('Returning to San Antonio...')
        game.travel_to('San Antonio', 0)
    else:
        # Show destinations and get choice
        current_city.display_destinations()
        num_destinations = len(current_city.get_destination_names())
        choice = get_valid_int(f'Choose (1-{num_destinations}): ', 1, num_destinations)
        # ... convert choice to city name, get distance, travel

items: In the else branch, use get_valid_int() from your function library — no crashes on bad input!
After the loop ends, display final stats using game.display_stats() and show the full route

ifYouGetStuck: Test each class separately first — get Game working before connecting it to City
Check your imports — from city import City (not import city)
Dictionary lookup pattern — cities[game.get_current_city()] gets the City object
Submit to CodeGrade early — it tests each class independently
Come to Zoom or office hours — multi-class programs can be tricky to debug
